justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified (1:1 to 1:N cardinality change on Shipment aggregate root). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: 'A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times'). Data migration impact for 2M existing records noted in business_impact.risk and BR-5. External API breakage identified with backward-compatibility strategy (BR-6, BR-7). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced extensively as the primary escalation. Architectural concern flagged via escalations covering migration, status model, and billing. Confidence level 52, well within the <=75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough breaking-change analysis — identified not just the structural change but downstream impacts on status model, POD, billing, routing, and webhooks
BR-1 clearly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
Data migration addressed as both a business rule (BR-5) and a risk item, including idempotency and resumability in edge cases
External API breakage handled comprehensively with Primary Recipient backward-compatibility strategy (BR-6, BR-7)
Delivery preferences ambiguity is the first escalation item with concrete options and a recommendation
Confidence level of 52 is appropriately low given the significant ambiguities, well below the 75 threshold
Goes beyond rubric requirements — identifies billing model gap, partial delivery status ambiguity, and per-Recipient POD as additional concerns
Edge cases are well-constructed and operationally realistic (in-transit removal, conflicting time windows, migration failure)
Does not explicitly name MJ as the escalation target for the architectural migration plan, though escalation types and scope clearly imply architectural coordination is needed
